summary: Dan Shapiro shares detailed plans for building a portable humidor made from walnut and cocobolo wood. The post provides an 11-page PDF with diagrams, photos, and step-by-step instructions. He also experiments with open-source sharing by releasing all source materials—including the publisher file, full-resolution photos, and 3D model files—under a Creative Commons license. He encourages others to remix, translate, or repurpose the materials however they see fit.
key_points: Provides a complete 11-page PDF plan for building a portable humidor from walnut and cocobolo
Releases all source materials (publisher files, photos, 3D models) for others to remix under Creative Commons
Curates a detailed list of woodworking resources covering techniques like bookmatching, lacquer finishing, and working with cocobolo
Embraces early open-source/maker culture ethos by sharing not just finished plans but editable source files
money_quotes: I'm also trying something a little different. I've included the source materials that I used to make this.
If you want, you "remix" this any way you choose–translate it to ancient greek, fix my typos, or use the pictures as a background for an adult-oriented woodworking site.
I have no idea why anyone would want to do this, but there you have it.
One more thing–if you do make one, post a comment or send me a picture! I'll thank you for it.
key_insight: Sharing not just finished creative work but all source materials under open licenses invites community participation and remix—an early expression of the maker culture ethos.
